message:
First, no it isn't. That it happens to women and has been given a name says nothing at all about who else it may or may not happen to. You may as well argue that the term "actress" is built around the idea that there are no male thespians.

The OP wasn't referring to this sub. It's quite reasonable to say, here, "This is what I would need to believe x. CMV.", but it's not in general. No one is obligated to explain the world to you.

But the entire concept of mansplaining is built around the idea that only men do it to women. If everyone did it to everyone, it wouldn't be called mansplaining. So he is asking for arguments that show that men do it to women far more often than in any other relationship. 

This sub isn't just for convincing someone against an idea, but also for convincing them to believe in one. They clearly have not accepted it as true, otherwise they wouldn't require proof.

message:
I'm not exactly a spokesperson for the body positivity movement, but I'm assuming that their aim is to alleviate the stigmatisation of obesity or something. Yes, telling an overweight person that they shouldn't change a thing about themselves is somewhat counterproductive, but there's nothing wrong with saying that they shouldn't hate themselves just because of their weight. That they shouldn't be ashamed of themselves. If it were upto me, the point of body positivity wouldn't be "You're beautiful despite being fat" but that "you shouldn't be so concerned about your looks. Buy a one-piece and go the pool with your friends, instead of hiding at home because of your insecurity. Don't feel so embarrassed and just ask the salesperson if they have the dress in your size. Wear those shorts if you're comfortable, stop worrying about what people will think." 

Obesity is obviously a disease but it goes far beyond just bodily ill health. Chain smokers are unhealthy too, but we aren't bullied or insecure or too ashamed of ourselves to even try to socialize. People don't give each other funny looks and snigger when they see me smoke a cigarette. 

Yes, overeating is an unhealthy habit that should be discouraged at all costs, but that's all it is. A disease. An addiction just like smoking. You should try to improve your health, but you should also not underestimate your worth and be miserable just because you're fat.

a $200-300 bonus for all employees is better than nothing, but I think there are many companies where he payout would be much greater for the employees. for example, [this article](https://www.cnbc.com/2018/05/09/how-much-the-5-highest-paid-ceos-earn.html) says the CEO of CBS made a salary of $69.3 million and that the CEO of Broadcom made $103.2 million in 2017. according to Forbes, CBS has 16,730 employees, and Broadcom has 14,000 employees (sorry for not having 2017 numbers). cutting the salary of these CEOs to a generous $250,000/year, that’s $69.05 million and $102.95 million extra.

$69,050,000/16,730 employees = $4,127.32/employee

$102,950,000/14,000 employees = $7,353.57/employee

as shown here, there are certainly cases where cutting the CEO’s salary would be very helpful to thousands of people. additionally, I think this should be scaled to help the lower income employees more than those next in line under the CEO. if this kind of redistribution happened nationwide or worldwide, it would mean a lot of money back in the hands of lower and middle class people who work hard everyday to just make ends meet.

Throwing money at people doesn't fix poverty. 

I'm sorry for such a low effort post, but that's the only relevant thing I can think of to respond with. I can somewhat agree that such a large gap between the rich and poor is not a good thing, however the problem is not able to be addressed in a fundamentally effective manner.

message:
I can't speak to the entirety of your post, but I think I can address some of the rationale behind the name "Black Lives Matter". Like many people have said, the statement Black Lives Matter is *not* intended to say that white/other lives do not matter. Rather, it is simply drawing attention to a specific inequality.  

Take a step back from the politically charged nature of "Black Lives Matter" for a moment, and think about the idea of Mother's Day. When you celebrate Mother's Day, does it mean that on that day you forget about your father, grandfather, and other family members? Does it mean you think those other family members are not worth celebrating?

Of course not. Even in the midst of celebrating your mom, you don't stop loving your other family members. All you are doing is taking that specific moment (a Sunday in May) to recognize your mother in particular. 

It's the same with the name "Black Lives Matter". The name isn't saying that other lives have stopped mattering while black people fight against police brutality. It isn't invalidating other inequalities that exist within society. It is highlighting the particular issue of violence against black people, and trying to create recognition for the problem.

message:
I'm not exactly a spokesperson for the body positivity movement, but I'm assuming that their aim is to alleviate the stigmatisation of obesity or something. Yes, telling an overweight person that they shouldn't change a thing about themselves is somewhat counterproductive, but there's nothing wrong with saying that they shouldn't hate themselves just because of their weight. That they shouldn't be ashamed of themselves. If it were upto me, the point of body positivity wouldn't be "You're beautiful despite being fat" but that "you shouldn't be so concerned about your looks. Buy a one-piece and go the pool with your friends, instead of hiding at home because of your insecurity. Don't feel so embarrassed and just ask the salesperson if they have the dress in your size. Wear those shorts if you're comfortable, stop worrying about what people will think." 

Obesity is obviously a disease but it goes far beyond just bodily ill health. Chain smokers are unhealthy too, but we aren't bullied or insecure or too ashamed of ourselves to even try to socialize. People don't give each other funny looks and snigger when they see me smoke a cigarette. 

Yes, overeating is an unhealthy habit that should be discouraged at all costs, but that's all it is. A disease. An addiction just like smoking. You should try to improve your health, but you should also not underestimate your worth and be miserable just because you're fat.

I am deeply concerned about the prospect of a President doing something horrible simply because it polls well. There have been numerous historical instances of popular policies being implemented to disastrous effect. What will Trump do when the experts say one thing and the polls say another?

From history? Invading Iraq in 2003. The three strikes law when it was implemented. Alcohol prohibition in 1920. The anti-communist witch hunts of the 50s. De-regulating the housing market in the 80s and 90s. The point is that just because something is popular, it doesn't make it a good idea. A president that only seeks to do what is popular rather than what is best is incredibly dangerous.

message:
But fat people do take care of themselves. We keep clean, we shower, we brush our teeth and hair, we moisturize. Tess Holliday probably spends more time grooming herself, doing makeup, and choosing clothing everyday than you and me put together. 

You just have a particular standard for beauty. Your standard is that thin = beautiful. That's not true for everybody and you're being unfair and wrong when you insist that people who aren't thin aren't taking care of their bodies. This isn't about health for you, it's about beauty, so please stop pretending you're worried about fat people's health. If you were worried about fat people's health, you'd also worry about our mental health which is damaged and endangered by cruelty and shaming. 

You're allowed to find thin people beautiful. You're allowed to find thin people, on the whole, more beautiful than fat people. That's a personal aesthetic opinion and that's find. What's unfair and hurtful is when you generalize your personal aesthetic opinion to everyone else while pretending that you're worried about fat health.
personality_characteristics: ['Healthy', 'Democrat', 'Fascinated with the latest health and fitness gadgets']
